# Extended Case References — Lecture r3NzM4MlAl8

## Cases mentioned in passing

### Oakland Bay Bridge steel tie rod hydrogen cracking
- **canonical_cluster_id:** "Oakland Bay Bridge steel tie rod hydrogen cracking"
- **Anchor:** `§7.p7`
- **Quote (layer 3):** "I meant to bring — Don Sadoway, a faculty member of this department, sent me a link to a problem they're having on the Oakland Bay Bridge. They have some big steel tie rods, and they're corroding and breaking, and they're holding this bridge up. It's a multi-billion-dollar bridge. It's hydrogen cracking, because it doesn't show up right away — it takes a few days to show up. It's due to corrosion. Another way to get hydrogen into the steel is not just a welding arc plasma — it can be by a corrosion process. You can take a bolt that has no welds, stick it in seawater, put a stress on it, and..."

### General Dynamics Quincy shipyard LNG tanker problems
- **canonical_cluster_id:** "General Dynamics Quincy shipyard LNG tanker problems"
- **Anchor:** `§8.p3`
- **Quote (layer 3):** "So when I was working on the skirts to hold up these big aluminum tanks for the LNG tankers down at Quincy — they were building them at Quincy — that's what I had to do. I had to measure each one of those locations along the weld. I had to weld a plate a foot long just to get all the specimens out of it, because you had big tensile specimens and everything else."
